sentiment.rationale: King Ahasuerus displays imperial excess; Queen Vashti is deposed for refusing to appear before the court.
Esther is chosen queen through a providential sequence; Mordecai uncovers a plot against the king.
Haman's rage at Mordecai leads him to decree the annihilation of all Jews throughout the Persian empire.
The Jews mourn in sackcloth; Mordecai challenges Esther to act, and she resolves to approach the king at the risk of her life.
Esther is received by the king and hosts a banquet; meanwhile Haman builds gallows for Mordecai in his pride.
A providential reversal: the king honors Mordecai, forcing Haman to publicly exalt the man he sought to destroy.
Esther reveals Haman's plot; the king's wrath falls on Haman, who is hanged on the gallows he prepared for Mordecai.
The king issues a counter-decree allowing the Jews to defend themselves; joy and gladness spread throughout the provinces.
The Jews prevail over their enemies; the feast of Purim is established as a perpetual memorial of deliverance.
Mordecai is elevated to second in the kingdom, seeking the welfare of his people and speaking peace.

missingCharacters.name: Ahasuerus
missingCharacters.description: King of Persia (identified with Xerxes I) who reigned over 127 provinces from India to Ethiopia and chose Esther as queen.

missingCharacters.name: Zeresh
missingCharacters.description: Wife of Haman who advised him to build gallows for Mordecai, then warned him of his impending downfall.

missingCharacters.name: Harbonah
missingCharacters.description: One of the king's chamberlains who pointed out the gallows Haman had built for Mordecai.

missingCharacters.name: Memucan
missingCharacters.description: One of the seven princes of Persia who advised King Ahasuerus to depose Queen Vashti.

missingCharacters.name: Vashti
missingCharacters.description: Queen of Persia who was deposed by Ahasuerus for refusing to display herself before the court at a royal feast.

summaries.summary: King Ahasuerus holds a great feast in Shushan; Queen Vashti refuses to appear before the court and is deposed by royal decree.
Esther, a young Jewish woman raised by Mordecai, is chosen queen; Mordecai discovers and reports a plot against the king's life.
Haman, angered by Mordecai's refusal to bow, persuades the king to issue a decree for the destruction of all Jews in the empire.
Mordecai mourns and urges Esther to intercede with the king, declaring she may have come to the kingdom for such a time as this; Esther resolves to go before the king.
Esther approaches the king, finds favor, and invites him and Haman to a banquet; Haman meanwhile prepares gallows to hang Mordecai.
The king discovers Mordecai was never rewarded for saving his life and commands Haman to publicly honor Mordecai, humiliating him.
At Esther's second banquet, she reveals Haman's plot to destroy her people; the king orders Haman hanged on his own gallows.
Esther and Mordecai receive Haman's estate; the king issues a new decree allowing the Jews to defend themselves, bringing great joy.
The Jews defend themselves and defeat their enemies throughout the provinces; the feast of Purim is established to commemorate the deliverance.
Mordecai is elevated to second in command under King Ahasuerus, using his position to promote the welfare and peace of his people.

crossReferences.note: Haman's plot to destroy the Jews echoes the principle that those who curse Abraham's seed will themselves be cursed.
Mordecai's declaration of providential timing parallels the New Testament theme that God works all things for good.
Esther's willingness to risk death for her people parallels Daniel's resolve to pray despite the king's decree.
Haman is hanged on his own gallows, fulfilling the proverb that those who dig a pit will fall into it.
The celebration of Purim with feasting and gifts to the poor parallels Nehemiah's call to share with those who have nothing.

verses.reason: The king loves Esther above all women and sets the royal crown upon her head.
Mordecai's challenge: who knoweth whether thou art come to the kingdom for such a time as this—one of the OT's most quoted verses.
Esther's courageous resolve: if I perish, I perish—a model of sacrificial faith.
The dramatic irony as Haman assumes the king wishes to honor him, setting up his own humiliation.
Esther pleads for her life and the life of her people before the king at the fateful banquet.
The Jews experience light, gladness, joy, and honor—a powerful statement of providential reversal.
Purim established: days of feasting, gladness, sending portions, and gifts to the poor.
